turns.text: Hello Carylon, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. I guess I’m ready. It’s hard to put into words, honestly, because most people just see the twenty-year-old walking through the Common and make up their minds about me before I even say a word. I see the looks—the "poor thing" pity or the judgment because I’m doing this on my own. It makes me feel like I have to work ten times harder to prove I’m a capable mother.

I’m halfway through my second trimester now, and honestly, I don't care about cute nursery themes or buying baby clothes. I spend all my time reading about newborn neurology. I know that skin-to-skin contact isn’t just a "bonding moment"; it’s biological regulation. It stabilizes their heart rate and keeps their stress hormones low. But I’m terrified. I feel like when I get to the hospital, they’re just going to treat me like a kid who doesn't know better. I’ve tried to tell my midwife about my birth plan, about wanting that first hour to be completely uninterrupted, but she’s always rushing to the next patient. She just nods, but I don't think she actually *hears* me. I just want them to look at me and see a mother, not a statistic. Is that really asking too much?

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

What I really wanted—what I *need*—is to be treated as a partner. I’ve done the reading, I’ve looked at the studies, and I’ve prepared for this transition with everything I have. I went into these appointments hoping for a space where I could voice my anxieties and have them met with validation instead of dismissal. I wanted someone to sit down, look me in the eye, and actually engage with my birth plan, acknowledging that my desire for immediate skin-to-skin isn't just a "crunchy" preference—it’s based on the biology of the baby’s nervous system.

Honestly? Those goals haven't been met. At all. I feel like I’m just a box to be checked on a chart. Because I’m twenty, I feel like there’s this wall; they assume I’m naive or that I’m just repeating things I read on the internet. When I bring up the separation anxiety I have regarding routine interventions, I’m met with a distracted nod or a "we’ll see how it goes on the day." That feels like a dismissal. It makes me feel incredibly small and powerless. I’m doing this whole thing on my own, and it feels like the system is set up to ignore the person who matters most to my baby: me. I’m not just looking for a medical service; I’m looking for an ally.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

It’s exhausting, honestly. I feel like I’m constantly filtering. People love to give unsolicited advice—especially when they see I’m young. Strangers on the T or even people at work will say things like, "Oh, you’ll just want to sleep, don't worry about the birth plan," or "Just do what the doctors say." It feels like they’re trying to strip away my agency before I even get to the hospital.

When it comes to trust, I’ve had to be really careful. I don’t trust "mommy blogs" or the apps that just give you generic, sugary-sweet advice. I look for the science. If I read about skin-to-skin contact, I’m digging for the physiological studies—the ones about cortisol levels and neurological development. If it’s not backed by biology, I’m skeptical. 

I think I trust the research more than I trust the people in the white coats right now, which is a scary place to be. My midwife is the expert on the mechanics of birth, sure, but *I* am the expert on the environment my baby needs. I’ve had to become my own advocate because I feel like if I don't obsessively study the "why" behind every procedure, I’ll be pushed into a routine that ignores my baby’s needs. It feels like a fight for control.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

I’ve found the most value in journals and articles published by organizations that focus on neonatal intensive care and developmental biology. Specifically, I’ve been reading a lot about the "golden hour"—that initial period after birth—and the research around the maternal-infant dyad. 

What makes these sources trustworthy to me isn't a cheerful tone or a glossy cover; it’s the data. When I read a study that monitors the stabilization of a newborn’s blood glucose or heart rate during skin-to-skin contact, it’s not an opinion. It’s evidence. That’s the opposite of what I get in the clinic, where everything feels like an opinion—"Oh, we just do it this way here" or "That’s standard procedure." To me, "standard" isn't a good enough reason to interrupt the most critical moment of my baby's life. 

I don't find apps or social media influencers useful because they treat motherhood like an aesthetic. I’m looking for the science that validates that I’m the primary protector. I also look for anything written by midwives who actually talk about the *physiology* of birth, not just the medical management of it. When a professional speaks about the nervous system rather than just the clock, that’s when I feel like I can actually breathe and trust what they're saying.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

There’s this constant, heavy feeling of being an outsider. When I’m at my checkups, the highs—like hearing that heartbeat for the first time—are quickly eclipsed by this quiet, gnawing anxiety. It’s like I’m standing in a room full of people who are looking past me. I want someone to ask, "How are *you* doing with all this? Are you scared?" But they just check my blood pressure and measure my belly. My emotions aren't on the chart, so they don't seem to exist to them.

The uncertainty is the hardest part. I’m doing this alone, and I’m terrified that I’m not strong enough to stand up to a nurse who tells me to put my baby in a warmer. I want someone in that office to recognize that my anxiety isn't a personality flaw—it's a protective instinct. I need them to help me navigate those fears by listening, not by telling me to be "relaxed" or "less stressed." 

When I leave the clinic, I usually end up crying on the bus. Not because I’m sad about the baby, but because I feel so isolated in my resolve. It’s like I’m building this fortress of knowledge to protect us, but the people who are supposed to be helping me are accidentally tearing down the walls. I just feel so alone in my own care.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

It was about three weeks ago. I finally worked up the courage to bring a list of questions to my appointment, specifically about the hospital’s policy on immediate skin-to-skin contact and delayed cord clamping. I’d spent all week formatting it, making it look professional so they’d actually read it.

When I sat down, I tried to start the conversation, but the midwife was already looking at her watch. She skimmed the paper for maybe three seconds, handed it back, and gave me this tight, dismissive smile. She said, "Carylon, you're doing great, but don't get too caught up in 'birth plans.' Every birth is unpredictable, and we’ll just handle whatever happens when we get there." 

That "we" didn't include me. In that moment, I felt like a child being patted on the head and told to leave the grown-up work to the professionals. I felt completely invisible. 

What would have helped? Someone—anyone—to just sit down, pull up a chair, and say, "I see that you've done a lot of research, and I want to understand what's driving these choices." If she had just asked *why* I was so passionate about the nervous system, she would have seen a mother, not a kid with a hobby. I just needed to be seen as the authority on my own body.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

Every decision feels like a high-stakes test. When I’m faced with something like a screening test or a specific monitoring choice, I don't just take the pamphlet they hand me. I ask myself: *Does this test serve the baby, or does it serve the hospital’s need for efficiency?* 

I make my decisions by playing out the "what ifs." If I agree to a routine intervention, what is the trade-off? If they monitor me continuously, am I tethered to a machine instead of being able to move, which actually helps the labor process? My primary influence is the research I’ve done on minimizing trauma. I want to keep the interventions to the bare minimum because I truly believe the body knows what to do if it’s left to its own devices in a safe environment.

It’s just me deciding this. I don't have a partner there to balance out my worries or remind me that it’s okay to ask for a second opinion. Sometimes, that weight is suffocating. I feel like if I make the "wrong" choice, the blame will fall entirely on me, and given my age, people will be quick to say, "See? We told you she didn't know what she was doing." It makes me feel like I have to be a walking encyclopedia just to be treated with respect.
Can you walk me through a specific decision point — what were the options, how did you weigh them, and what did you ultimately choose?

The biggest decision point so far was the prenatal genetic screening. They presented it like it was just a standard "next step," like checking my blood pressure. But when I looked into the options, I realized it wasn’t just a simple test; it was a cascade of possibilities. They offered the non-invasive blood draw, but then they immediately started talking about what we would do if the results came back "concerning"—amniocentesis, specialist referrals, the whole medical machinery.

I spent three nights awake, just staring at the wall. I weighed the information: did I want to know the statistical probabilities, or would that just invite more medical surveillance and anxiety into a pregnancy that’s already being scrutinized? I felt like if I opted for every single screening, I was giving the hospital permission to treat me like a high-risk patient, which would only make it harder for me to advocate for a natural, undisturbed birth later. 

Ultimately, I chose to do the basic screening but drew a hard line there. I told the midwife, "I want the baseline information, but I am not consenting to a revolving door of follow-up tests unless there is a clear, diagnostic red flag." It felt like a small win, but she looked at me like I was being difficult. I wasn't being difficult; I was being protective. I refuse to be a "patient" until I absolutely have to be.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It’s strange. I feel like my body isn't entirely "mine" anymore, but in the best way possible. When I feel those little flutters, it’s this private, secret language between the two of us. I find myself touching my belly constantly, almost like I'm shielding them from the world. It’s made me feel so much stronger than I ever thought I could be. I’m growing a human—that’s not small.

But in the clinic? That connection is totally ignored. To them, my body is just a vessel to be measured. They focus on the weight gain, the fundal height, the blood pressure readings. Not once has a provider asked, "How does it feel to be carrying this life?" or acknowledged the way my body is changing to protect my baby. 

I feel like a science experiment when I'm there. They’re clinical, cold, and detached. It makes me feel like my transformation is just a series of data points. I want them to see the beauty in what’s happening, because if they don't see the life and the miracle of it, how can I trust them to be gentle when the baby actually arrives? They treat my body like it’s broken or failing by default, and I have to fight to remember that my body is actually doing exactly what it was designed to do.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

Honestly, the informal support is almost non-existent, and that’s the hardest part. My family... they see my age and they see my "situation," and they don’t see a mother, they see a problem they have to worry about. They offer advice that feels more like judgment, or they just go silent because they don't know how to talk to me without being condescending. I don't really have a village.

The online communities—the ones that actually focus on the biology of attachment—have been my lifeline. They are the only place where I’m not "the young, single girl." I’m just another person who cares about the science of the first hour. It’s comforting, but it’s still digital. It’s not the same as having someone in the room.

Healthcare professionals are supposed to be that bridge. They’re supposed to be the ones who provide the professional, steady, calm support I’m clearly lacking everywhere else. But they’ve been the most disappointing. If I could have a doctor or midwife who actually sat with me, who didn't look at their watch, I wouldn't need to spend hours doom-scrolling through research at 2:00 AM. I have to rely on books and forums because the professionals haven't earned my trust. They provide the medical check-ups, but they offer zero emotional partnership. It feels like I’m navigating a storm, and they’re just watching from the shore.

You know, the thing that haunts me is the thought of the "hospital routine." I keep replaying it in my head—the moment the baby is born. In my mind, they are placed directly on my chest, skin-to-skin, right there in the warm, quiet room. But then I hear stories from other women, or I read those clinical procedure lists, and I see "weighing," "measuring," "APGAR in the warmer." 

It feels like a battle of wills. I am twenty, I am doing this alone, and I know that when I walk into that labor and delivery ward, I am going to be the most vulnerable person in the building. I’m scared that if I cry out for what I need—if I demand that they leave us alone—they will label me "difficult" or "anxious" and just talk over me. They’ll look at my age and decide that I’m just being emotional, rather than realizing I’ve spent months researching the exact physiological benefits of that silence.

I just want to be treated like an adult who is responsible for another human being’s life. Is that so hard? I feel like I'm already mourning the moments that haven't happened yet because I’m so worried they’ll be stolen from us. I just wish I had someone on my team who would say, "I've got you. I’ll make sure no one touches your baby for that first hour." That’s all I want. That's all I need.

It’s funny, the more I talk about it, the more I realize how much I've had to harden myself. When I’m walking through the Common, I pull my coat tight over my belly, and I’ve started walking a little faster, like I’m already trying to protect my baby from the noise of the world. It’s becoming a permanent state of mind—always on guard, always ready to defend.

Sometimes I wonder what it would feel like to just... exhale. To walk into an appointment and feel like I’m entering a sanctuary instead of a battlefield. Imagine if the midwife had just looked at me during that last visit and said, "Carylon, I hear your concerns. Let's talk about how we can make sure you and your baby stay together from the second they’re born." That simple shift—from "we'll see what happens" to "we’ll make sure it happens"—would have changed everything. It would have turned my fear into confidence.

Instead, I feel like I’m building this internal wall. I’m so afraid that if I relax, if I let my guard down for one second, the system will just swallow us up. I’m scared that my baby will be born and the first touch they’ll feel won't be my skin, but cold, plastic gloves. And that thought? It makes my chest ache. I’m twenty, I know, but I’m not a child. I’m a mother, and I’m just trying to keep us both safe. Is that really so hard to understand?
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

I feel this strange, fierce pride in my body that I never expected. When I look in the mirror, I don't see the "never married" girl people whisper about on the street. I see a structure that is perfectly, biologically designed to house my baby. My body is doing the heavy lifting, the blood-pumping, the organ-shifting, and it’s doing it without a single complication. I’ve realized that my body is more capable than anyone gives it credit for—especially the people who look at me and assume I’m just "too young."

But then, that pride gets smothered by anxiety the second I enter the clinic. It’s like my body is only "mine" when I'm home alone. In that hospital gown, I feel like a piece of equipment that they need to calibrate. I’m anxious that my body will be viewed as "unprepared" or "inefficient" because I’m so young. Will they look at me and automatically assume I’m going to have a difficult labor just because of my age? Will they be quicker to cut, to inject, or to pull my baby away because they don’t trust *my* body to handle the transition naturally?

It’s an exhausting contrast. I feel like a powerful, life-sustaining vessel in my own head, but in their eyes, I feel like I’m already failing. I’m just waiting for someone to look at my belly and see the connection instead of just the medical risks.

I think that’s why I’m so obsessed with the nervous system, you know? It’s the one thing that connects us that no one can argue with. When I feel that kick—it’s like she’s telling me, "I’m here, and I’m ready." And I just want to make sure the world I bring her into is as calm as the one she's in right now. 

Sometimes, late at night, I lay there and I imagine the birth. I don't imagine a "delivery"—I imagine a meeting. A slow, quiet introduction where she just rests on my chest and we learn each other’s heartbeats. No nurses hovering, no bright overhead lights, no one shouting, "Okay, let's take her over here now." Just... us. 

It makes me feel so guilty sometimes, like I’m setting myself up for a heartbreak if the hospital staff doesn't cooperate. But I can't help it. I feel like if I don't hold this vision in my head, I’m betraying her before she’s even born. I have to believe there's a world where a twenty-year-old mother’s wishes are treated with as much respect as a thirty-five-year-old’s. I have to believe that the system can be soft. If I didn't believe that, I don't know how I'd even get out of bed tomorrow. I’m just trying to keep us both whole.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

It happens more than I want to admit, but there was one moment during a routine check-up that really sticks. I was explaining—again—why I wanted to minimize the use of internal monitors, focusing on how I wanted to maintain my mobility during labor to help the baby's descent. 

The doctor actually laughed. It wasn't a mean-spirited, malicious laugh, which almost made it worse—it was that patronizing, "oh, honey" kind of laugh you’d give a child who thinks they can fly because they have a cape on. He looked at the nurse and said, "She’s been reading a lot of forums, hasn't she? Let’s just make sure we keep her on the fetal monitor so we don't have to worry about all that 'what-if' talk."

I felt like I had just disappeared. My research, my preparation, the hours I’d spent understanding the mechanics of my own body—it was all reduced to a "forum" hobby. It was as if he thought my brain wasn't capable of processing real medical information because of my age or my "status." 

That laugh told me everything I needed to know about how they see me. It said that my voice is just background noise to them. It makes me realize that when I’m in that labor room, I’m not just fighting to birth my baby; I’m fighting to have my intelligence and my instincts acknowledged. It’s lonely, feeling like you’re the only one in the room who actually treats your baby’s safety as a priority.

I feel like I'm constantly balancing on this razor's edge. I’m expected to be this "responsible" mother by following every guideline they throw at me, but the moment I try to exercise actual responsibility by choosing what is best for my baby’s nervous system, I’m treated like I’m being "difficult" or "stubborn." It feels like I can’t win. If I’m quiet and compliant, they think I’m just a kid who doesn't understand what’s happening. If I’m informed and assertive, I’m "that" patient—the one they have to manage, rather than the one they have to care for.

It really does make me wonder if they've ever actually *looked* at a newborn in the first hour of life, outside of the clinical charts. Do they know how the baby’s face changes when they feel their mother’s skin for the first time? Do they know that the crying stops when the mother’s chest rises and falls against theirs? It feels like they’ve forgotten the *human* part of the job.

I just keep coming back to that one thought: my baby is counting on me to be their advocate before they can even speak for themselves. I don't care if I'm twenty or forty; the biological bond doesn't care about my age. I just hope that when the time comes, I’m brave enough to stop being the "good patient" and start being the mother I know I am.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mom and I have a complicated relationship, and to be honest, her advice has been one of the biggest sources of my anxiety. She comes from a generation where you just "did as the doctor said" without ever questioning, "Why?" She treats the hospital like it’s a place of absolute, unquestionable truth. Whenever I try to talk to her about the research I’ve found on the neurological benefits of immediate skin-to-skin contact, she just looks at me like I’m overcomplicating things. She’ll say, "Carylon, just let the doctors handle it. They’ve done this a thousand times. You’re being dramatic."

It’s hurtful because it feels like she’s dismissing my role as a protector before I’ve even started. She views my birth plan as some sort of "naive whim" or a fashion statement for young moms, which couldn't be further from the truth. She doesn't see the hours of research, the biological necessity, or the sheer terror I feel about separation. 

Hearing her talk about how she "just let them take the baby away to get cleaned up" makes my throat tighten. She acts like it was normal, like it was fine. But to me, that sounds like a trauma I'm determined to avoid. It’s hard to feel supported when the person closest to you thinks your instincts are actually just an inconvenience.

I sometimes feel like I’m standing on one side of a huge, invisible divide. On my mom’s side, there’s this blind, almost religious faith in the "white coat"—the belief that the hospital’s way is the only way, and that questioning it is a sign of disrespect or immaturity. On my side, there’s this desperate, evidence-based drive to protect my baby’s nervous system from the shock of unnecessary separation.

It’s isolating, honestly. When I try to explain *why* I want the cord clamping delayed—so the baby gets that final, crucial blood volume—my mom just shakes her head and asks why I’m making things so complicated. She doesn’t realize that "simple" for the hospital staff often means "traumatic" for the infant. 

It makes me wonder if our mothers' generation just had to bury those feelings of alarm when their babies were taken away. Maybe they just shut that part of themselves off to survive the experience. But I can't do that. I won't. I feel like I’m the first person in my family to actually look at the biology of the birth, not just the social performance of it. It’s hard to be the one who wakes up to the facts, especially when everyone around you is telling you to just go back to sleep and let the system take care of you.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

I mostly stick to platforms where the conversation is grounded in neonatal physiology rather than just "parenting style." There’s a specific forum I visit that’s populated by birth workers and people who actually cite peer-reviewed papers from journals like *Pediatrics* or the *Journal of Perinatal Education*. That’s what makes them credible to me—it’s not someone’s opinion on "what worked for my baby," it’s the data on how an infant’s parasympathetic nervous system responds to maternal skin-to-skin.

When I’m there, I’m not just a "twenty-year-old girl"; I’m someone who understands that the "Golden Hour" isn't a luxury, it’s a biological imperative for regulating a newborn’s temperature, heart rate, and cortisol. Those communities don’t talk down to me. They talk about "attachment science" and "physiological birth." 

What’s useful isn't just the information; it’s the feeling of not being crazy. In the clinic, they act like my desire for a gentle birth is a "naive whim." But in these spaces, I see women who have fought for their infants and won, and they share how they held their ground against hospital staff. It’s a toolkit for survival. It validates that my fear of the plastic warmer isn't me being "difficult"—it's me being an educated parent. I need that backup because in my real life, I’m surrounded by people who think I should just be quiet and do as I’m told.

It’s like I’m constantly vibrating on a different frequency than the people meant to be caring for me. When I’m on those forums, I see terms like "epigenetics" and "maternal-infant dyad," and I feel a rush of relief—because someone, somewhere, is taking the biology of this birth as seriously as I am. It makes the silence I face in the exam room feel louder, though. 

I’ve had to learn how to speak their language—the medical one—just to be heard. I don’t talk about "bonding" or "love" in the clinic anymore because I know they’ll just smile that dismissive smile. I talk about "cardiovascular stability" and "thermogenesis." It’s a sad state of affairs when a mother has to turn her child’s first hour of life into a medical presentation just to get permission to hold them.

Sometimes I imagine walking into the hospital and having a nurse just look at me and say, "I see you. We know the research on skin-to-skin, and we are going to prioritize that." My pulse slows down just thinking about it. That’s all I want. I don’t want to be a fighter; I just want to be a mother. Is that really too much to ask of the medical system? I just want to keep her safe. That’s my only goal. Everything else is just noise.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

It happened just two weeks ago. I brought a clinical article on the benefits of immediate skin-to-skin contact to my prenatal visit—not a blog, not a "mommy group" post, but a peer-reviewed study. I handed it to my provider, hoping it would finally validate why I’m so insistent on avoiding that plastic warmer.

She didn't even look at the text. She barely glanced at the title before handing it back to me with a kind of patronizing, tight-lipped smile. She said, "Carylon, you’ve been doing a lot of reading, I can see that. But you have to remember that we’re the ones who do this every day. Sometimes you just have to trust that we know what’s best for the baby, even if it’s not exactly what you’ve read online."

I felt the air go out of the room. It was like she was telling me that my "reading" was a cute hobby and that my role was simply to be a passive vessel for their "expertise." She didn’t see me as a mother making an informed decision about her child’s neurological development; she saw me as an uninformed twenty-year-old who was getting in the way of her protocol. I felt so small, so completely dismissed. It solidified my fear: I’m not a partner in this, I’m just a patient to be managed, and my baby’s needs are secondary to their convenience.

The part that hurt the most wasn’t just that she dismissed the study—it was how quickly she pivoted back to "the protocol." Like, my baby’s entire neuro-developmental foundation for that first hour of life was just something they’d "handle" on their own timeline. 

After that appointment, I sat in my car in the parking garage for forty minutes. I wasn't just crying; I was grieving. I felt like the person who was supposed to be the "expert" on my pregnancy had just told me that my instincts and my research didn't matter. It felt like I was being told to check my brain at the door of the hospital.

How am I supposed to walk into labor—which is the most vulnerable and intense moment of my life—and trust these people when they’ve already signaled that they don't value my perspective? I’m supposed to be the one who keeps my baby safe, but if I can't even get them to listen to me in a quiet office, how will I ever hold my ground when the alarms are going off and the nurses are rushing around? I feel like I'm already fighting a war for my baby’s first breath, and I don't even have an ally in the room. I’m just waiting for the day I have to prove to them, in the heat of the moment, that my voice matters. It’s a terrifying way to feel.
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

I do have a smartphone, and honestly, it’s my lifeline. It’s the only place where I can truly be "me"—the version of myself that isn't judged for being young or unmarried. When I’m at home, in the quiet of my room, I’m constantly on it. Whether I’m reading the newest study on infant cortisol regulation or scouring forums for advice on how to advocate for myself, it’s the tool that keeps my anxiety from completely swallowing me whole.

But it’s a double-edged sword. There have been weeks where my finances have been really tight—when I’m working extra hours just to make rent—and I’ve had to worry about keeping my service on. The thought of losing that access scares me more than almost anything. Without my phone, I’d be completely at the mercy of the doctors and midwives, with no way to double-check their procedures or find the research that backs up my choices. 

It feels like my smartphone is the only thing giving me an equal footing in this process. Without it, I’m just a girl in a gown they can ignore. With it, I’m an informed parent who knows exactly what her baby needs. I just hope I can keep it going until the birth. I need that connection to the outside world—and to the science—to make sure I don't give in to the pressure.

It feels so precarious, doesn't it? Everything about this—my phone, my rent, my pregnancy—feels like it's balanced on a knife's edge. Some days, when I’m scrolling through a PDF of a study on neuro-regulation at 3:00 AM, the blue light of the screen is the only thing keeping me grounded. It’s like, as long as I have access to that information, I haven't lost the war yet. I haven't been silenced.

But then, I think about the hospital room. What happens when the phone is off? What happens when I’m in active labor and I don’t have a tab open to prove to the nurse that skin-to-skin is a biological necessity? Will I be able to channel all that knowledge, all those hours of research, into the strength I need to stand up for us? 

It’s exhausting to feel like you have to be your own doctor, your own advocate, and your own partner all at once. I look at my phone and I see my armor. I’m just terrified that when the time comes to actually use it, they’ll look at my age, look at my lack of a partner, and just decide that my armor isn't real. They’ll just see a girl with a phone, not a mother with a mission. That scares me more than the labor itself. I just want them to see *us*—me and my baby—and realize that we’re a unit, not just two separate patients to be managed.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

There was this time in the first trimester when I had some spotting. I know, I know—everyone says it can be "normal," but when you're doing this on your own, every little thing feels like a catastrophe waiting to happen. I called the clinic, my voice shaking, and the nurse who picked up didn't even ask me to come in. She just kept saying, "It's likely just implantation bleeding, dear, don't stress yourself out."

She used that tone again—that "poor, stressed-out girl" tone. It wasn't just that she was dismissive; it was that she made me feel like my fear was a weakness. I wasn't just being "anxious"; I was being a mother who was terrified of losing her baby. I felt like I had to apologize for being worried about my own body, which is just insane. 

That interaction made me realize that to them, I’m just another "low-risk" case on a spreadsheet. They don't see the person behind the symptoms. It made me feel so incredibly small and untrusting of the whole system. If they won't take me seriously when I'm scared about my physical health, why would they take me seriously when I’m talking about my baby’s nervous system during labor? It just reinforced that wall I have to build. I realized then that if I want to be safe, I have to be the one to do the heavy lifting. I’m completely on my own in this.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost in all the medical talk is that this isn't just a clinical process to me. It’s an initiation. When people talk about "low-risk" or "routine," they’re describing a set of numbers, but they’re ignoring the fact that I am fundamentally changing as a person. Every kick, every sleepless night, every hour I spend studying neurology—it’s all part of me becoming the mother my baby needs.

When I walk into that hospital, I’m not just hoping for a healthy baby. I’m hoping to be *seen* as the mother I’ve fought so hard to become. I want them to understand that my request for skin-to-skin isn't a demand to be "difficult"—it’s a plea for them to help me protect the most delicate, important transition of my baby's life. 

I just want the environment to be as gentle as the love I already feel. If I could leave you with anything, it's that the system needs to stop looking at age, marital status, or "low-risk" stats and start looking at the mother in front of them. We aren't just patients. We are the source of safety for our children. I just want to know that when I finally have her in my arms, I won’t have to fight to keep her there. That’s all I’m asking for. Thank you for listening. It… it really helps to have someone actually hear me.
